mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the High and Late Middle Ages in western Europe including feudalism, the church, the Black Death, and political developments.
Relevant to subsection A1b on the commercial effects of the Crusades and the broader crusading context of Louis IX's campaigns in subsection A3a.
Directly covers subsection B1a on the early Renaissance in Italy including Humanism, new techniques in art, and the city-states.
Covers subsections A2 and B4 on the Holy Roman Empire, the Investiture Controversy, and the Hohenstaufen and Habsburg emperors.
Directly covers subsections A5 and B7 on England from the Norman Conquest through Edward I and the later Plantagenets.
Directly covers subsection A5a on the Norman Conquest and introduction of feudalism under William I.
Directly relevant to subsection A5b-iii on King John's conflict with the barons and the granting of Magna Carta.
Relevant to subsection A1d on the church in medieval society including papal hegemony, reform movements, and the Inquisition.
Relevant to subsection B5 on Renaissance Italian political thought and the Florentine republic under the Medici.
Directly relevant to subsection A1e-iii on Latin and vernacular literature in the High Middle Ages.
Relevant to subsection B7 on English culture in the late Middle Ages and the flowering of vernacular literature.
Relevant to subsection B5 on Italian literature and cultural developments in the late medieval period.
Directly relevant to subsection A1e-ii on Scholasticism and developments in philosophy and theology.
Directly relevant to subsection A1e-ii on Scholasticism, as Aquinas was its foremost representative.
Relevant to subsection A6 on the establishment of the Scandinavian kingdoms of Denmark, Norway, and Sweden.
Relevant to subsections A1a and B2b on the feudal nobility, its declining importance, and the growing influence of the bourgeoisie.
Relevant to subsections A5c and B7b on Scotland's relations with England and the wars of independence.
Relevant to subsection A1f on the status of Jews in medieval society, their economic role, persecutions, and migrations.
Relevant to subsection A1f on Jewish persecutions and subsection B8c on the Inquisition and treatment of Jews in Spain.
Directly relevant to subsection B2b on the Black Death (1347-50) and its economic, social, and political effects.
Relevant to subsection B8c-d on Spanish and Portuguese explorations and colonial policy in the Age of Discovery.
Relevant to subsection B4a on the Habsburg emperors and the enhancement of Habsburg power in Europe by 1500.
Relevant to subsection A1d on reform movements in the medieval church including Cistercians and mendicant orders.
Relevant to subsection B1c on late medieval intellectual developments including the topic of witchcraft.
Relevant to subsection B8c on Spanish explorations and territorial acquisitions in the New World.
Relevant to subsection B3 on the conciliar movement and reform movements that presaged the Reformation.
Relevant to the numerous competing dynasties -- Capetian, Plantagenet, Hohenstaufen, Habsburg, Visconti, Medici -- discussed throughout the section.
